question: Based on Table 5.2-10 LTOP setpoints and Table 5.3-6/7 P-T limits, is there adequate margin between LTOP actuation and the P-T curves?

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It cites guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as relevant ASME code cases for materials, quality assurance, and inspection procedures for pressurized water reactors.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with regulatory requirements for overpressure protection, referencing specific ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases as mandated by 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC conditions, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0015.title: Reactor Vessel Materials
nuscale_ch05_0015.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel, serving as a key barrier to fission product release. The RPV and its components are constructed from materials specified by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant’s design life.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) comply with relevant regulatory requirements, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria, by designing, fabricating, and testing reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class 1 standards. It also clarifies the classification of connected piping and states that the 2013 Edition of the ASME BPVC is the code of record for the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the use of ASME and NRC methodologies to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides these limits for various operating and testing conditions to ensure protection against non-ductile failure.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing real-time pressure and level data to control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a critical pressure boundary to prevent the release of fission products and provides structural support for various reactor components. Materials used for the RPV are selected according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure long-term integrity and safety.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored through RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, ensuring reliable detection to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design prioritizes maintaining coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support plant safety during normal and abnormal conditions. This system is integral to each NuScale Power Module and is designed to meet stringent performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures in place to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation over its 60-year design life, and confirms that all criteria remain within regulatory limits.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various system elements, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

edges.description: Section A provides references to regulatory and industry standards that underpin the technical and regulatory basis for overpressure protection, while Section B describes the implementation and compliance with these standards in the context of overpressure protection provisions.

Section A provides references, including the NuScale FSAR Section 5.2, which is directly cited in Section B's description of the reactor vessel and its relationship to the reactor coolant system, establishing a structural cross-reference between the two sections.

Section A lists references that include ASME and EPRI documents, some of which are the same codes and standards that Section B states are complied with for the design and construction of the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the technical basis for the analyses and requirements described in Section B regarding reactor vessel pressure-temperature limits and weld examinations.

Section A outlines the overpressure protection provisions and regulatory compliance, while Section B describes the reactor vessel as the pressure boundary component to which the LTOP setpoints and P-T limits (from the referenced tables) apply, connecting them through the shared focus on reactor vessel integrity under pressure transients.
Section A provides details about the specific ASME BPVC code cases and compliance provisions referenced in Section B's general statement of compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC requirements.

Section A discusses compliance with overpressure protection requirements and references applicable ASME codes, while Section B provides details on pressure-temperature limits and vessel integrity per ASME code, thus Section A's LTOP setpoints must be evaluated against Section B's P-T limits to determine if regulatory margin is maintained.

Section A describes the reactor vessel materials and their role as pressure-retaining components, while Section B states that these components comply with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements, connecting the vessel's physical properties to its regulatory compliance.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor vessel and its role in the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies detailed requirements and analyses for pressure-temperature limits and structural attachments relevant to the vessel's integrity, thus Section B provides specifications for the general vessel described in Section A.

Section A establishes that the reactor pressure vessel and its components comply with ASME BPVC requirements, while Section B discusses specific ASME BPVC provisions for attachments and welds, showing that the design and fabrication details in B are governed by the compliance framework set in A.

Section A describes the design and function of the pressurizer, which is relevant to understanding LTOP setpoints, while Section B lists references that may provide supporting standards or guidelines for the integrity and operation of the reactor coolant boundary, including the pressurizer.

Section A describes the pressurizer's function in maintaining and communicating reactor coolant system pressure, which is directly relevant to Section B's discussion of overpressure protection requirements that the pressurizer system must help satisfy.

Section A describes the pressurizer's function in maintaining and communicating pressure within the reactor vessel, which directly affects the pressure conditions that Section B's reactor vessel materials must withstand, thus linking the operational pressure control (A) to the structural integrity requirements (B) relevant to LTOP and P-T limits.

Section A describes the reactor vessel as a pressure-retaining component of the reactor coolant system, while Section B lists references that provide technical and regulatory standards relevant to the integrity and quality assurance of the reactor coolant boundary, which includes the reactor vessel.

Section A describes the reactor vessel as the pressure-retaining component whose integrity is protected by overpressure protection provisions detailed in Section B, which outlines compliance with regulatory codes for overpressure protection.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in rapidly communicating pressure changes within the reactor vessel, which is structurally integrated as described in Section B's overview of the reactor vessel and its components, establishing that the pressurizer and reactor vessel are physically and functionally connected in the pressure boundary relevant to LTOP and P-T limits.

Section A discusses the inability to distinguish specific leakage sources in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B lists references that may provide standards or guidelines relevant to leakage detection and integrity, indicating that Section A's discussion may be informed by or based on the documents cited in Section B.

Section A discusses the detection of reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage, which is relevant for ensuring the integrity of the boundary that Section B aims to protect through overpressure protection measures, thus both sections address different aspects of maintaining RCPB integrity but do not directly connect LTOP setpoints or P-T limits.

Section B describes the reactor vessel as a component of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is the subject of leakage detection discussed in Section A, establishing that the leakage detection methods in Section A apply to the vessel described in Section B.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system design, while Section B lists references that support the technical and regulatory basis for the integrity and operation of that system, including relevant standards and guidelines.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor coolant system design, which is the system protected by the overpressure protection measures discussed in Section B, establishing the context for the regulatory requirements and code compliance described in Section B.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B gives specific details about the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which is a component of the RCS described in Section A.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure compliance with pressure-temperature limits, while Section B lists references that provide technical standards and guidelines supporting the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, thus Section B supports the claims and requirements described in Section A.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature (P-T) limits are not exceeded, while Section B addresses compliance with ASME and NRC requirements for overpressure protection, both of which are connected by the need to maintain reactor vessel integrity through adherence to regulatory limits on pressure and temperature.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure compliance with pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, which are established based on the reactor vessel's material properties and design described in Section B.

Section A discusses the inability to distinguish specific leakage sources within the containment due to the unique RCS design described in Section B, which omits traditional components like pumps and external piping.

Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure compliance with pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, which are directly related to the LTOP setpoints discussed in Section A as both are measures to protect reactor vessel integrity under various operating conditions.

Section A describes operational procedures for system components, while Section B lists references that provide standards and guidelines relevant to those procedures, indicating that Section A's actions may be informed by or compliant with the standards cited in Section B.

Section A describes system operational steps and component states relevant to pressure control, while Section B outlines regulatory compliance for overpressure protection, connecting them through the need for system operations (A) to satisfy the overpressure protection requirements (B) when evaluating LTOP setpoints versus P-T limits.

Section A describes operational procedures and system components relevant to pressure and water level management, while Section B details the reactor vessel's structural role and references sections (5.1 and 5.2) that include the LTOP setpoints and P-T limits, thus Section B structurally references the context provided in Section A.
